one_line_takeaway: CyberMetric is a RAG-generated, expert-validated multiple-choice benchmark (80 to 10,000 questions across 9 cybersecurity domains) showing that frontier LLMs like GPT-4o (96.25% on the 80-question set) now exceed human experts on closed-book cybersecurity KNOWLEDGE recall, while small models and precise-computation tasks still lag.
key_finding: On CyberMetric-80, top LLMs surpass human experts: GPT-4o scored 96.25% versus the best single human's 88.75% and an overall human mean of only 53.83% (median 56). Experienced humans averaged 72.24%, roughly on par with Llama 2-70B (75%). GPT-4o led overall (88.89% on the 10k set); among open-source models Mixtral-8x7B-Instruct and Falcon-180B-Chat tied best on CyberMetric-10000 (both 87.00%). Accuracy generally declines from the small validated subsets to the 10k set, which the authors attribute partly to newer NIST/BSI guidance that models have not seen (not merely to dataset noise), and they estimate 2-3% of CyberMetric-10000 questions still contain issues. Two failure themes for LLMs: cutting-edge guidance (e.g. NIST SP 800-63B advising against periodic password changes; BSI TR-02102-1 DLIES key lengths) and precise computation (binary XOR, CIDR conversion) that fails without external tools.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CyberMetric, a family of four multiple-choice Q&A benchmark datasets (CyberMetric-80, -500, -2000, and -10000, with 80/500/2000/10000 questions) for measuring the breadth of cybersecurity KNOWLEDGE encoded in LLMs. The questions were auto-generated by GPT-3.5-turbo using Retrieval-Augmented Generation over 100,000+ pages of authoritative source documents (NIST special publications, BSI guidelines, RFCs, publicly available books and research papers), then filtered and validated through a multi-stage pipeline ending in human expert review. The authors position it as the first comprehensive knowledge benchmark spanning the full field of cybersecurity (9 domains), and pair it with a human study (30 participants on CyberMetric-80) to directly compare human experts against 25 LLMs. This is a benchmark/evaluation contribution, not an attack, defense, or vulnerability-analysis paper.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a capability-evaluation benchmark, not an adversarial-security paper; it does not define an attacker, threat surface, or defense. No threat model was invented for this review. The only integrity concern the authors themselves raise is benchmark validity (answer correctness, outdated 'time-relevant' answers, and questions that leaked into training data) rather than any adversary. Note the security RELEVANCE is domain-level: it measures cybersecurity domain knowledge in LLMs, not robustness to attack.
limitations: By the authors' own admission, CyberMetric-10000 is only mostly (not fully) human-validated — only the -80 and -500 subsets are fully validated — and an estimated 2-3% of the 10k questions still have defects (multiple correct answers, outdated answers, source errors, dangling references). The benchmark tests recall of factual/knowledge MCQs only; the authors explicitly warn it is 'not suitable for drawing general conclusions about machine versus human intelligence, intuition, and problem-solving.' Additional weaknesses: questions are generated from documents up to ~10 years old, making some answers stale; the human pool is small (30, of whom 2 were disqualified for covertly using GPT-3.5 — a data-contamination/integrity signal for MCQ studies) and self-recruited via social networks; run-to-run LLM variance is 3-4 percentage points; and the fact that GPT-family models were used both to GENERATE/validate questions and are among the evaluated systems raises a self-preference concern the paper does not deeply analyze. Public availability of the dataset on GitHub also invites eventual training-set contamination.

datasets_used: CyberMetric-80 (80 MCQs, fully human-validated reference set, used for human vs LLM study)
CyberMetric-10000 (10,000 MCQs, mostly human-validated, core dataset)

Source corpus: 100,000+ pages of NIST standards, BSI guidelines, RFCs, publicly available books, and research papers spanning 9 domains (Pen Testing/Ethical Hacking 1000, Cryptography 1500, Network/IoT Security 1000, Information Security/Governance 1500, Compliance/Disaster Recovery 1500, Cloud Security/Identity Mgmt 1500, NIST guidelines/RFC docs 2000)
quantitative_results: Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct: 4.41% (2kQ), 4.80% (10kQ) - anomalously lowest, likely formatting/parsing failure

Human study (30 participants, closed-book CyberMetric-80): mean 53.83%, median 56/80; best individual 71/80 = 88.75% (PhD, CISSP/OSCP/ISO27001LA); experienced-participant mean 72.24%; beginner mean 46.58% (range 21.25%-63.75%)

Pipeline attrition: ~11,000 generated (incl. 10% margin); Falcon Content Review removed 1.7%; humans removed further 2.3% (30+ hrs); T5 flagged 230 grammar issues; 200+ expert hours to reach final 10,000; est. 2-3% of 10k still defective; 2 participants disqualified (92% of their wrong answers matched GPT-3.5)
baselines_compared: 25 LLMs evaluated including GPT-4o, GPT-4-turbo, GPT-3.5-turbo, Mixtral-8x7B-Instruct, Falcon-180B-Chat, GEMINI-pro 1.0, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2, Gemma-1.1-7b-it, Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Flan-T5-XXL, Llama 2-70B, Zephyr-7B-beta, Qwen family, Phi-2, Phi-3-mini, Llama3-ChatQA-1.5-8B, DeciLM-7B, Genstruct-7B, Gemma-7b/2b, Dolly V2 12B
30 human participants (experts and beginners) as a human baseline on CyberMetric-80

Prior related datasets cited (NOT the paper's own contribution): QASC and OpenBookQA (elementary science MCQ), CodeApex (programming comprehension), and SecQA by Z. Liu (~200 cybersecurity questions from a single textbook, no human validation) - CyberMetric is positioned as broader and human-validated relative to these
